verdict_reasoning: VEGFA is a secreted growth factor that exists in two canonical forms: (1) a soluble secreted form released into the extracellular space and circulating serum, and (2) membrane-associated forms. The protein is transcribed as a precursor and undergoes alternative splicing producing multiple isoforms. Critically, VEGFA is tethered to the cell surface and extracellular matrix via heparan sulfate proteoglycans (HSPGs) and neuropilins, creating a stable, non-diffusible surface reservoir. The membrane-proximal isoforms (particularly VEGFA121 and shorter variants) retain heparin-binding domains and remain surface-associated post-secretion through wash-resistant interactions with HSPGs. Additionally, VEGFA binds stably to its canonical receptor VEGFR on the cell surface, and the protein is a well-characterized target of therapeutic antibodies (bevacizumab, others) and recombinant receptor-Fc fusion proteins (aflibercept) that engage VEGFA in the extracellular compartment, including on the cell surface. The surface-tethered pool is biologically relevant for juxtacrine signaling and local concentration effects. While much of the secreted VEGFA diffuses away, a substantial fraction remains anchored to the cell surface via HSPG binding and remains accessible to extracellular binders.
